title: Grass Seed Calculator per m² 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Grass Seed Calculator: How Much Per Square Meter?
description: Find the right amount of grass seed per m² for your lawn. Choose seed type and calculate total grams needed. Free tool.
intro: This calculator tells you exactly how many grams (or kilograms) of grass seed you need to cover a given area in square meters. The core formula is simple: **Total Seed (g) = Area (m²) × Seeding Rate (g/m²)**. Seeding rates vary widely by grass species — from as low as 15 g/m² for fine fescues to 50 g/m² or more for tall fescue blends — so getting the math right prevents costly overseeding or patchy, thin coverage. Use this calculator before purchasing seed for new lawns, overseeding bare spots, or establishing sports turf, where precision directly impacts germination density and establishment costs.
keyTakeaway: **Total Seed (g) = Area (m²) × Seeding Rate (g/m²)** — e.g., 100 m² of Kentucky Bluegrass at 20 g/m² = 2,000 g (2 kg). Always add 10–15% overage for slopes, edges, and uneven soil.
useCases: Establishing a brand-new residential lawn of 250 m² with a tall fescue blend requiring 35 g/m² — calculate you need 8.75 kg before buying bags at the garden center.
Overseeding a worn sports field of 1,200 m² with perennial ryegrass at 30 g/m² to recover turf after a heavy-use season, budgeting for 36 kg of seed.
Repairing bare patches totaling 18 m² in a Kentucky Bluegrass lawn after a drought, using the standard 20 g/m² rate to buy only the small quantity needed.
Planning a low-maintenance meadow of 500 m² with a fine fescue mix at 15 g/m², determining that 7.5 kg is sufficient and avoiding over-purchasing expensive seed blends.
Seeding a rooftop green or commercial courtyard of 80 m² with a shade-tolerant grass mix (25 g/m²), calculating 2 kg to fit a tight contractor budget.
explanation:
## How It's Calculated

The fundamental formula used by this calculator is:

```
Total Seed (g) = Area (m²) × Seeding Rate (g/m²)

To convert to kilograms:
Total Seed (kg) = Total Seed (g) ÷ 1,000

Recommended purchase quantity (with 12% overage buffer):
Purchase Qty (kg) = Total Seed (kg) × 1.12
```

The **seeding rate** is the most critical variable. It is set by the grass species, whether you are doing a **new seeding** (higher rate) or **overseeding** (typically 50–75% of the new-seeding rate), and soil conditions. Seeding rates below assume good seedbed preparation on level ground.

---

## Reference Table

Seeding rates for common grass types under US conditions, based on cooperative extension and USDA recommendations:

| Grass Type | New Seeding (g/m²) | Overseeding (g/m²) | Season | Sun Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Kentucky Bluegrass | 15–20 | 8–12 | Cool | Full sun |
| Perennial Ryegrass | 25–35 | 15–20 | Cool | Full sun / Part shade |
| Tall Fescue | 30–40 | 15–25 | Cool | Full sun / Part shade |
| Fine Fescue (blend) | 12–18 | 8–12 | Cool | Shade tolerant |
| Bermudagrass (hulled) | 10–15 | 5–8 | Warm | Full sun |
| Zoysiagrass | 8–12 | 5–8 | Warm | Full sun |
| Buffalograss | 20–30 | 10–15 | Warm | Full sun / Drought tolerant |
| Annual Ryegrass | 30–40 | 20–25 | Cool | Full sun |

> Sources: USDA NRCS Plant Materials Program; Penn State Extension; University of California ANR.

---

## Typical Examples

**Example 1 — New residential backyard lawn (Tall Fescue)**
- Area: 150 m²
- Grass type: Tall Fescue blend, new seeding
- Rate: 35 g/m²
- Total seed: 150 × 35 = **5,250 g (5.25 kg)**
- With 12% buffer: 5.25 × 1.12 = **5.88 kg → buy two 3 kg bags (6 kg)**

**Example 2 — Overseeding a sports field (Perennial Ryegrass)**
- Area: 800 m²
- Grass type: Perennial Ryegrass, overseeding
- Rate: 18 g/m²
- Total seed: 800 × 18 = **14,400 g (14.4 kg)**
- With 12% buffer: 14.4 × 1.12 = **16.1 kg → buy two 10 kg bags (20 kg)**

**Example 3 — Shaded side yard (Fine Fescue)**
- Area: 40 m²
- Grass type: Fine Fescue blend, new seeding
- Rate: 15 g/m²
- Total seed: 40 × 15 = **600 g (0.6 kg)**
- With 12% buffer: 0.6 × 1.12 = **0.67 kg → buy one 1 kg bag**

---

## Common Mistakes

1. **Using the same rate for overseeding as for new seeding.** Overseeding into existing turf requires only 40–70% of the new-seeding rate, since existing grass competes with seedlings. Using the full rate wastes seed and money.

2. **Ignoring germination percentage on the seed label.** A bag labeled "85% germination" means 15% of seeds won't sprout. If you need 2,000 live seeds per m², you must sow more seed to compensate. Always check the seed tag required by the Federal Seed Act.

3. **Not accounting for slopes and irregular edges.** A flat 100 m² calculation on paper often translates to 108–115 m² of actual surface area on a sloped lawn. Add at least 10–12% to your total for this reason.

4. **Mixing cool-season and warm-season grasses in the same purchase.** Bermudagrass and Kentucky Bluegrass have fundamentally different germination temperature requirements (soil temp >65°F vs. 50–65°F respectively). Blending them leads to patchy establishment.

5. **Sowing too deep.** Most grass seeds should be covered by no more than 3–6 mm of soil. Over-tilling or over-raking buries seeds too deep, dramatically reducing germination regardless of the quantity sown.

6. **Forgetting to adjust for seed purity.** Seed bags list both germination % and purity %. A bag at 90% purity means 10% of the bag's weight is inert matter or weed seed — factor this in for precision seeding on high-value projects.

---

faq.q: How many grams of grass seed do I need per square meter for a new lawn?
faq.a: It depends on the grass species. Kentucky Bluegrass requires 15–20 g/m² for a new lawn, while Tall Fescue needs 30–40 g/m² due to its larger seed size. Perennial Ryegrass falls in the middle at 25–35 g/m². Always check the seeding rate printed on the seed bag, which is required by the Federal Seed Act (7 U.S.C. § 1551) to appear on every label sold in the US.

faq.q: Is overseeding rate different from new seeding rate?
faq.a: Yes — overseeding into existing turf typically uses 40–70% of the new-seeding rate. For example, if Tall Fescue is seeded at 35 g/m² for a new lawn, overseeding the same species into thin turf requires only 15–25 g/m². The existing grass canopy shades newly germinating seedlings, reducing the effective establishment area and making it wasteful to apply full new-seeding rates.

faq.q: What soil temperature do I need for grass seed to germinate?
faq.a: Germination temperature varies by species. Cool-season grasses (Kentucky Bluegrass, Fescues, Ryegrass) germinate best when soil temperatures are 50–65°F (10–18°C), typically in fall or early spring. Warm-season grasses (Bermuda, Zoysia, Buffalograss) require soil temps above 65°F (18°C), generally late spring through summer. The USDA NRCS and university extensions recommend using a soil thermometer at 1-inch depth to verify conditions before seeding.

faq.q: Why does the calculator recommend adding a 10–15% buffer to the seed quantity?
faq.a: Several real-world factors reduce effective coverage: slopes increase true surface area vs. flat-map area (a 20° slope adds ~6% surface area), uneven soil texture causes seed to accumulate in depressions, birds and wind carry seed away, and hand-spreading is rarely perfectly uniform. A 12% buffer is a standard industry recommendation from turf professionals to ensure you don't run short mid-project.

faq.q: How do I read a grass seed label to verify quality?
faq.a: The Federal Seed Act mandates that every grass seed bag sold in the US must disclose: (1) pure seed percentage, (2) germination percentage, (3) weed seed percentage, (4) other crop seed percentage, and (5) inert matter percentage. A high-quality turf seed will show ≥95% purity and ≥85% germination. Multiply purity × germination to get the 'effective seed' fraction — e.g., 97% × 88% = 85.4% of the bag weight is viable seed.

faq.q: Can I seed in summer or do I have to wait for fall?
faq.a: For cool-season grasses (Fescues, Bluegrass, Ryegrass), late summer to early fall (August–October in most US zones) is the optimal window: soil is still warm enough for germination but air temperatures are cooling, reducing heat stress on seedlings. Spring seeding is a second option but competes with weed germination. Summer seeding of cool-season grasses is generally not recommended because soil temps above 75–80°F inhibit germination and seedling survival rates drop significantly.

faq.q: How long does it take grass seed to fully establish after seeding?
faq.a: Germination occurs in 5–30 days depending on species: Perennial Ryegrass is fastest at 5–10 days, Kentucky Bluegrass is slowest at 14–30 days, and Tall Fescue falls in between at 7–12 days. However, full establishment — meaning the turf can withstand normal foot traffic and mowing — takes 6–10 weeks for ryegrass and fescues, and 3–6 months for Kentucky Bluegrass. Avoid mowing until grass reaches 3–4 inches in height.

faq.q: Does seed depth affect how much seed I should use?
faq.a: Seed depth affects germination rate, not the quantity sown. Most grass seeds should be placed at 3–6 mm depth (no more than ¼ inch). If seeding by broadcast spreader, light raking or a slit-seeder pass ensures seed-to-soil contact without burying seeds too deep. Poor seed-to-soil contact — not insufficient quantity — is the most common cause of low germination rates in home lawn projects according to extension agronomists.
